---
title: "Foreign Bodies"
format: html
---

## Echogenecity of foreign bodies

As opposite with X-rays where many materials are radiotransparent, almost all foreign bodies are echogenic:

## Image acquisition

The vast majority of foreign bodies are superficial, and better visualised with **linear, high-frequency probes**.

Superficial foreign bodies may be very superficial and image quality can be improved by using:

-   A thick layer of gel
-   A stand-off pad (e.g. fluid bag)
-   A water bath technique

![Water bag technique. From [ACEP Sonoguide](https://www.acep.org/sonoguide)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/water-bag.jpg){fig-align="center" width="450"}

![Water bath technique. From [ACEP Sonoguide](https://www.acep.org/sonoguide)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/water-bath.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

## Typical foreign body appearance

Most foreign bodies appear as:

-   Bright (hyperechoic) structures
-   Well-defined margins
-   Posterior acoustic artifacts

The exact appearance depends on the material.

### Metallic foreign bodies

Metal produces:

-   Intense hyperechogenicity
-   Clean posterior acoustic shadow
-   Reverberation artifacts
-   Comet-tail artifact

![Metallic foreign body (broken needle). From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-metal-broken-needle.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

### Glass

Glass is highly reflective and usually demonstrates:

-   Bright echogenic margins
-   Posterior acoustic shadowing
-   Reverberation artifact

Small fragments may generate less prominent shadows.

![Glass foreign body. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-glass-1.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

![Glass foreign body. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-glass-2.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

### Wood

Wood is often more difficult to identify.

Typical findings include:

-   Linear hyperechoic structure
-   Variable posterior shadowing
-   Progressive surrounding hypoechoic halo caused by inflammation

The inflammatory halo becomes more evident after 24–48 hours.

![Wood foreign body. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-wood-1.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

![Wood foreign body. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-wood-3.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

![Almost iso-echogenic wood foreign body. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-wood-bamboo.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

### Plastic

Plastic usually appears as:

-   Hyperechoic linear structure
-   Variable acoustic shadowing
-   Less conspicuous artifacts than metal

Visualization depends on composition.

## Secondary sonographic signs

Secondary findings often help identify subtle foreign bodies.

### Hypoechoic halo

A hypoechoic rim surrounding the object represents inflammatory edema or granulation tissue.

This sign becomes increasingly apparent with time and improves detection of radiolucent foreign bodies.

![Wood foreign body. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/foreign-body-wood-2.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

### Abscess

Retained foreign bodies commonly lead to abscess formation.

Look for:

-   Hypoechoic or complex fluid collection
-   Posterior acoustic enhancement
-   Internal debris
-   Hyperemia on color Doppler

![Subcutaneous abcess. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/abcess.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}

### Soft tissue edema

Diffuse edema appears as:

-   Thickened subcutaneous tissues
-   Increased echogenicity of fat
-   Cobblestone appearance

![Soft tissue edema. From [The POCUS Atlas](www.thepocusatlas.com)](/images/bones-soft-tissues/soft-tissue-edema.gif){fig-align="center" width="450"}
